title: ANOM Data
text:
FEDERAL CONSTITUTIONAL COURT

- 2 BvR 625/25 -

In the proceedings

on

the constitutional complaint

of Mr (...),

- authorised representative:
(…) -

against
a) the Order of the Federal Court of Justice

of 21 January 2025 - 1 StR 281/24 -,

b) the Judgment of the Mannheim Regional Court

of 31 January 2024 - 5 KLs 811 Js 8839/23 -

and on the application for a preliminary injunction

the Third Chamber of the Second Senate of the Federal Constitutional
Court,

with the participation of Justices
Langenfeld,

Fetzer

and
Offenloch

unanimously held on 23 September 2025

on the basis of § 93b in conjunction with § 93a of the Federal
Constitutional Court Act

in the version published on 11 August 1993 (Federal Law Gazette I
p. 1473):

The constitutional complaint is not admitted for decision.

As the constitutional complaint is not admitted for decision, the
application for a preliminary injunction becomes moot (§ 40(3) of the
Rules of Procedure of the Federal Constitutional Court).

R e a s o n s:

With his constitutional complaint, the complainant challenges his
criminal conviction and prison sentence, which were based on information
from the surveillance of his ANOM-communications obtained by way of
mutual assistance from the United States of America (USA) and used as
evidence, as well as the dismissal of his appeal against the conviction
on points of law. At the same time, the complainant seeks a preliminary
injunction suspending the detention order against him until these
constitutional complaint proceedings have concluded.

In its judgment of 31 January 2024, the Mannheim Regional Court
convicted the complainant of six counts of illicit trafficking of
narcotic drugs in not insignificant amounts and sentenced him to an
aggregate prison term of six years and six months. The confiscation of
the value of criminal proceeds in the amount of EUR 106,600 was also
ordered. The factual findings supporting the conviction were almost
entirely based on evidence from text messages from encrypted
ANOM-communications that could be attributed to the complainant. The
Regional Court verified the authenticity of the data on the basis of
reports by the Federal Criminal Police Office on the collection and
transmission of the data. During that process, the Regional Court found
no indications that the data had been falsified. The transmitted
messages and contents of the communications were found by the Regional
Court to be internally consistent.

The complainant appealed against this judgment on points of law.
In addition to a substantive complaint [...], he also raised procedural
complaints against the use of ANOM-communications as evidence. He
argued, with reference to additional information, that these
communications are inadmissible as evidence. The Public Prosecutor
General requested the rejection of the appeal on points of law. He
argued that none of the procedural complaints were lodged in an
admissible manner and that they did not have merit. In this regard, the
Public Prosecutor General presented the procedural circumstances of the
case, insofar as they concerned the collection of the ANOM-data abroad
and transmission to German law enforcement agencies.

With its order of 21 January 2025, the Federal Court of Justice
dismissed the complainant’s appeal on points of law as unfounded,
insofar as it pertained to the use of the ANOM-data as evidence. [...]
Concerning the (admissible) procedural complaints through which the
accused (who is the complainant in this proceeding) challenged the use
of messages sent through the ANOM-Messenger App as evidence, the Senate
referred to its judgment of 9 January 2025 - 1 StR 54/24 -, para. 10 ff.
The fact that the analysis of the copied data did not amount to mass
surveillance and did not occur without specific grounds was supported by
further factual findings regarding ANOM in the challenged judgment. It
held that the interference with the right to privacy of
telecommunications protected by Art. 10(1) of the Basic Law and the
general right of personality under Art. 2(1) in conjunction with
Art. 1(1) of the Basic Law was limited.

With his constitutional complaint, the complainant claims that
the challenged decisions violate his right to a fair trial in accordance
with the rule of law protected by Art. 2(1) in conjunction with
Art. 20(3) of the Basic Law and his general right of personality under
Art. 2(1) in conjunction with Art. 1(1) of the Basic Law. Furthermore,
he alleges a violation of his right to a lawful judge (Art. 101(1)
second sentence of the Basic Law). [...]

6-17

[…]

The constitutional complaint is not admitted for decision, because it
does not meet the prerequisites for admission pursuant to § 93a(2) of
the Federal Constitutional Court Act
(Bundesverfassungsgerichtsgesetz – BVerfGG). The constitutional
complaint does not have general constitutional significance (§ 93a(2)
letter a Federal Constitutional Court Act), nor is its admission
necessary to enforce the complainant’s constitutional rights within the
meaning of § 93a(2) letter b of the Federal Constitutional Court Act
(cf. Decisions of the Federal Constitutional Court, Entscheidungen
des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGE 90, 22 <24 ff.>; 96,
245 <248 ff.>). The constitutional complaint is inadmissible. It
does not satisfy the procedural requirements of substantiation as laid
out in § 23(1) second sentence, § 92 of the Federal Constitutional Court
Act.

To meet the requirements of § 23(1) second sentence, § 92 of the
Federal Constitutional Court Act, the complainant must present the facts
of the case which they claim amount to a violation of fundamental rights
in a coherent and substantiated manner (cf. BVerfGE 81, 208 <214>;
113, 29 <44>; 130, 1 <21>). Furthermore, the constitutional
complaint must consider the underlying ordinary law, analyse the
constitutional significance of the case and provide sufficient
substantiation for the assumption that a violation of fundamental rights
appears at least possible (cf. BVerfGE 28, 17 <19>; 89, 155
<171>; 140, 229 <232 para. 9>; 149, 346 <359
para. 23>). If the constitutional complaint is directed against
judicial decisions, a substantiated argument for a violation of
fundamental rights also requires a discussion of the reasoning
underlying the challenged decision (cf. BVerfGE 105, 252 <264>;
140, 229 <232 para. 9>; 149, 346 <359 para. 24>). In this
regard, the complainant must demonstrate in detail that the decision is
based on the alleged violation of fundamental rights (cf. BVerfGE 89, 48
<60>) and thus must refute all the reasons that form the basis of
the decision in its entirety in a substantiated manner (cf. BVerfGE 105,
252 <264>). If existing case-law of the Federal Constitutional
Court addresses the constitutional questions raised in the
constitutional complaint, the alleged violation of fundamental rights
must be substantiated in accordance with the constitutional standards
developed by the Court; a generalised claim of a constitutional
violation is insufficient in this regard (cf. BVerfGE 101, 331
<345 f.>; 123, 186 <234>; 130, 1 <21>; 140, 229
<232 para. 9>; 142, 234 <251 para. 28>; 149, 346 <359
para. 23>).

In addition, in order to enable the Federal Constitutional Court to
review the constitutional complaint without having to conduct further
enquiry, the submission of additional documents may be required
(cf. BVerfGE 93, 266 <288>; 149, 346 <360 para. 25>; Chamber
Decisions of the Federal Constitutional Court, Kammerentscheidungen
des Bundesverfassungsgerichts – BVerfGK 5, 170 <171>;
cf. also European Court of Human Rights (ECtHR), Mork v. Germany,
Judgment of 9 June 2011, no. 31047/04, 43386/08, §§ 37, 39). A mere
general reference to further documents is not sufficient, as it is not
the task of the Federal Constitutional Court to examine the documents
referenced and other annexes and search for constitutionally relevant
facts or submissions (cf. BVerfGE 80, 257 <263>; 83, 216
<228>; BVerfGK 19, 362 <363>).

If the complainant wishes to deviate from the findings of fact or
their interpretation by the ordinary courts, he must combine his
alternative submission of the facts with a constitutional challenge of
the facts established by the ordinary courts (cf. BVerfGE 83, 119
<124 f.>; Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the First Chamber
of the Second Senate of 1 November 2024 - 2 BvR 684/22 -, para. 45 with
further references).

Finally, the complainant’s submissions must be coherent and truthful
(cf. Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the Third Chamber of the
Second Senate of 11 July 2018 - 2 BvR 1548/14 -, para. 16 with further
references).

2. The constitutional complaint does not satisfy these requirements.
The complainant’s submissions do not support his claim of a violation of
his right to a fair trial in accordance with the rule of law protected
by Art. 2(1) in conjunction with Art. 20(3) of the Basic Law or his
general right of personality under Art. 2(1) in conjunction with
Art. 1(1) of the Basic Law (see a) below). Furthermore, it does not
support his claim of a violation of his right to a lawful judge in
accordance with Art. 101(1) second sentence of the Basic Law (see b)
below). The complainant did not conclusively demonstrate the possibility
of a violation of a fundamental right or a right that is equivalent to a
fundamental right.

a) Based on the complainant’s submissions, there is no indication
that the challenged decisions of the Regional Court or the Federal Court
of Justice resulted in a violation of his right to a fair trial
protected by Art. 2(1) in conjunction with Art. 20(3) of the Basic Law
or his general right of personality under Art. 2(1) in conjunction with
Art. 1(1) of the Basic Law. Contrary to the view of the complainant, the
use of the ANOM-data as evidence is not objectionable under
constitutional law.

aa) (1) In its case-law, the Federal Constitutional Court recognises
that a prohibition against the use of evidence is an exception that
requires justification under constitutional law. This is because a
prohibition limits the ability of law enforcement agencies to prove or
disprove the occurrence of a criminal act, which adversely affects the
central objective of criminal proceedings - reaching a decision that is
just and materially correct. Violations of fundamental rights which
occur outside of the main hearing do not necessarily warrant the
conclusion that the judgment delivered by the criminal court, which was
based on the findings in the main hearing, also violates constitutional
law and the right to fair trial or the general right of personality in
particular (cf. BVerfGE 130, 1 <28>). Consequently, it is also the
established case-law of the criminal courts that there is no fundamental
principle in criminal procedural law which stipulates that every
violation of the law on obtaining evidence automatically leads to an
inadmissibility of such evidence in the context of criminal procedure.
Consequently, the question whether a violation leads to an
inadmissibility of the evidence must be answered on a case-by-case
basis, taking into account the specific circumstances, especially the
nature of the violation and its weight in a balancing of the relevant
conflicting interests (‘Weighing of Interest Requirement’, cf. BVerfGK
14, 107 <111>; Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the First
Chamber of the Second Senate of 1 November 2024 - 2 BvR 684/22 -,
para. 97; -, Decisions of the Federal Court of Justice in Criminal
Matters, Entscheidungen des Bundesgerichtshofes in Strafsachen
– BGHSt 38, 214 <219 f.>; 44, 243 <249>; 51, 285 <289 f.
para. 20>; […]). A prohibition against the use of evidence is the
exception, not the rule, as it can only be assumed on the basis of an
explicit legal rule or due to significant and overarching reasons in an
individual case (cf. BGHSt 40, 211 <217>; 44, 243 <249>; 51,
285 <290 para. 20>). Assessing the consequences of a violation of
law in the process of obtaining information or bringing it into the
criminal proceedings and deciding whether such a violation leads to a
prohibition against the use of evidence is primarily the task of the
competent ordinary courts (cf. BVerfGE 130, 1 <31>; BVerfGK 4, 283
<285>; 9, 174 <196>; 14, 107 <111>; 18, 193
<203>; Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the First Chamber of
the Second Senate of 1 November 2024 - 2 BvR 684/22 -, para. 97).

(2) However, in an individual case, a prohibition against the use of
evidence may be required by the Constitution. Thus, as was correctly
pointed out in the constitutional complaint, a prohibition for
constitutional reasons may result from the fact that a violation of law
causes the accused to not have sufficient opportunity to influence the
course and outcome of the proceedings, that the minimum standards for
effectively establishing the truth are no longer met or that the use of
the information results in a disproportionate interference with the
general right of personality. Furthermore, the use of information which
was obtained in violation of law may not be permitted if this would
encourage the procurement of the evidence in violation of law. A
prohibition against the use of evidence may therefore be required in
case of severe, deliberate or objectively arbitrary procedural
violations that involved a calculated or systematic disregard of the
safeguards provided by fundamental rights (cf. BVerfGE 130, 1 <28>
with further references).

(3) Generally, the same applies to the use of evidence that was
obtained from sources outside of Germany and introduced into German
criminal proceedings (Federal Constitutional Court, Order of the First
Chamber of the Second Senate of 1 November 2024 - 2 BvR 684/22 -,
para. 97). Of course, this does not mean that prior to using evidence
obtained abroad through mutual assistance, the trial court in Germany is
required to verify the legality of the collection of the evidence
provided by the foreign state in order to be able to potentially
establish a (derivative) prohibition against the use of the evidence –
with a weighing the relevant interests if necessary – if the collection
of the evidence proves illegal. Rather, in accordance with the
established case-law of the Federal Court of Justice, which the
constitutional complaint does not dispute on constitutional grounds, the
courts of the requesting (German) state do not generally review the
sovereign decisions of the requested state by the standard of the
relevant laws of that state (cf. Federal Court of Justice, Order of
21 November 2012 - 1 StR 310/12 -, BGHSt 58, 32 para. 34 with further
references; Order of 9 April 2014 - 1 StR 39/14 -, Neue Zeitschrift
für Strafrecht – NStZ 2014, p. 608; Order of 2 March 2022 - 5 StR
457/21 -, BGHSt 67, 29 para. 26; Order of 9 January 2025 - 1 StR 54/24
-, juris, para. 18).

Furthermore, the trial court is generally not required to assess the
collection of evidence by the requested state by the standards of German
law, which from the outset are not applicable to the collection of
evidence abroad (cf. Federal Court of Justice, Order of 2 March 2022 - 5
StR 457/21 -, BGHSt 67, 29 para. 26). In the context of mutual legal
assistance, the structures and substantive content of foreign legal
orders and perspectives must generally be adhered to even if they are
not necessarily consistent with domestic views in an individual case
(cf. BVerfGE 113, 154 <162 f.>; BVerfG, Order of the Second
Chamber of the Second Senate of 4 December 2019 - 2 BvR 1258/19 and 2
BvR 1497/19 -, para. 55; Federal Court of Justice, Order of 2 March 2022
- 5 StR 457/11 -, BGHSt 67, 29 para. 26; Judgment of 9 January 2025 - 1
StR 54/24 -, juris, para. 18).

Evidence which has been obtained by way of mutual legal assistance
may, however, including for constitutional reasons, be inadmissible if
the evidence was obtained in the requested state in disregard of
national or European minimum standards regarding the rule of law. The
limits on the use of evidence established by the Federal Court of
Justice with reference to the national and European ordre
public are breached if the collection of evidence abroad did not
meet the indispensable minimum level of fundamental rights protection
and the minimum standards under international law insofar as it binds
the Federal Republic of Germany in accordance with Art. 25 of the Basic
Law. This is especially the case if the collection of evidence violated
the prohibition of torture or inhuman and degrading treatment or if it
caused an interference with the essence of fundamental rights in another
way relevant to human dignity. The essence of procedural fairness and
the principle of proportionality are also indispensable principles in
this regard (cf. Federal Court of Justice, Judgment of 9 January 2025 -
1 StR 54/24 -). As with the – considerably more intrusive and, with
regard to possible violations of fundamental and human rights by foreign
states, inherently riskier – case of extradition proceedings between
Germany and other states (cf. on this BVerfGE 109, 13 <35 f.>;
109, 38 <61>; 140, 317 <349 para. 68>; Federal
Constitutional Court, Order of the Second Chamber of the Second Senate
of 4 December 2019 - 2 BvR 1258/19 und 2 BvR 1497/19 -, para. 59), the
principle of mutual trust must also be observed when information
collected through telecommunications surveillance is obtained
(cf. Federal Court of Justice, Judgment of 9 January 2025 - 1 StR 54/24
-, juris, para. 29). In accordance with the principle of mutual trust,
it may be assumed that principles of the rule of law and human rights
protection have been observed in the foreign state, as long as this is
not refuted by the facts of the case (cf. BVerfGE 140, 317 <349
para. 68>).

On the basis of these standards, which themselves are not called
into question by the constitutional complaint, the challenges raised
therein are not persuasive.

To the extent that the complainant contends that the ordinary
courts did not sufficiently consider whether the procedures to obtain
the ANOM-data complied with indispensable constitutional principles in
accordance with Art. 79(3) in conjunction with Art. 1 and Art. 20(3) of
the Basic Law and a minimum level of fundamental rights protection
because ‘nearly nothing is known’ regarding the means by which the data
was obtained, this is contradicted by the complainant’s own statement of
the facts. According to this, it is known that at the direction of the
FBI, the software on ANOM-devices was equipped with a ‘backdoor’
function that ensured that every message sent via the messenger service
was sent to a so-called iBot server without the user’s knowledge, in the
form of an encrypted copy that could be uniquely matched to the
respective device. These messages would then first be decrypted by the
FBI in a temporary storage on that server, then re-encrypted into that
storage and finally forwarded to the transfer server with a few days’
delay. In this way, the communication of ANOM-users was supposed to be
monitored continuously. In accordance with the factual submissions of
the complainant, it is known that a still unknown EU Member State made
the iBot server available for the storage of messages on the basis of a
bilateral mutual legal assistance treaty with the USA and transmitted
them to the USA without decrypting and evaluating them itself. According
to the findings of the Federal Court of Justice, the process was
conducted by the third country on the basis of court orders obtained in
accordance with its procedural law. These findings may have been called
into question by the constitutional complaint, but could not be
challenged in a legally effectively way (cf. Federal Constitutional
Court, Order of the First Chamber of the Second Senate of 1 November
2024 - 2 BvR 684/22 -, para. 45).

The only information lacking is the identity of the state which
hosted the iBot server and the specific circumstances and content of the
court orders. However, in light of the standards laid out above, it is
not clear why this information is relevant to the present case. While a
review of these orders may have provided information as to whether the
retention and transmission of the data to the USA complied with the law
of the unknown EU Member State, in light of the standards laid out
above, that information is ultimately not relevant. It is also unclear
how deficiencies in the procedure or the content of these unknown court
orders could have led to the conclusion that the collection of ANOM-data
no longer complied with basic rule of law requirements. This is because,
according to the factual findings submitted, the circumstances of the
process of data collection as well as its extent, ultimately limited to
the users of ANOM-devices, was dictated by the FBI; the Member State
itself did not conduct an analysis of the data or made use of its
content. The Federal Court of Justice’s determination that as a
consequence, the courts orders could not have contributed to ‘mass
surveillance without specific grounds’ is also correct from the
perspective of constitutional law.

To the extent that the complainant contends in this context that the
principle of mutual trust could not apply in the present case because,
as a result of the secrecy on the part of the USA regarding the Member
State and the court orders issued in that state, the procedure of
obtaining the evidence was a ‘black box’ for him, he fundamentally
misunderstands the meaning and the – normal – consequences of the
‘distribution of the burden of proof’ that exists in this regard. If
there are no indications that the foreign state to which the request for
legal assistance was made could have violated the principles of the rule
of law and human rights protection when obtaining the evidence, it must
be assumed that this is not the case. If no special circumstances are
apparent which exceptionally lead to a prohibition against the use of
evidence, then the fundamental and, as shown above, constitutionally
required principle that all evidence introduced in the main hearing may
be used applies. In this regard, it is irrelevant whether the accused or
the competent (German) court was able to make a thorough assessment of
the collection of evidence abroad.

The factual findings submitted by the complainant also do not
support his further claim that, based on the facts as reported by the
USA, the procedure for collecting evidence abroad occurred in violation
of the minimum standards for the rule of law safeguarded by Art. 79(3)
in conjunction with Art. 20(3) of the Basic law and the indispensable
level of fundamental rights protection both in the USA (see a) below)
and in the unknown Member State of the EU (see b) below).

(aa) Regarding the procedure in the USA, the complainant first
claims that it is already questionable whether the FBI, as a law
enforcement agency, was even allowed to take action with regard to
crimes committed exclusively abroad under US law. For a number of
reasons, the complainant’s claims do not amount to a possible
constitutional violation. Firstly, this merely raises the question of a
violation of US law, without sufficiently substantiating it. Secondly,
the constitutional complaint does not demonstrate in a comprehensible
manner how such an alleged violation of US law by the FBI would justify
an assumption that the procedure in the USA violated the minimum
standards for the rule of law in accordance with Art. 79(3) in
conjunction with Art. 20(3) of the Basic Law. Thirdly, the
constitutional complaint does not demonstrate in a comprehensible manner
that, from the point of view of the USA, the procedure actually
concerned crimes committed exclusively abroad (see further on this
<b>, below).

(bb) Furthermore, the constitutional complaint seeks to establish a
violation of the minimum standards for the rule of law enshrined in
Art. 79(3) in conjunction with Art. 20(3) of the Basic Law on the basis
that the only purpose for conducting the surveillance of ANOM-devices in
a state other than the USA could have been to evade the limitations
imposed by the Fourth Amendment of the US Constitution (protection of
privacy) and by the Wiretap Act, which the complainant contends would
have applied if communications infrastructure in the USA had been used.
According to the constitutional complaint, the FBI thus evaded the
limitations placed on it by law. This argument is also not coherent. If
the provisions of US law cited in the constitutional complaint only
apply – as the complainant claims – to the access to communications
infrastructure in the USA, then telecommunications surveillance which
takes place outside of this infrastructure must be outside their scope
of application from the outset. The initial premise of the
constitutional complaint therefore does not demonstrate an unlawful
circumvention of the law, but merely that the FBI did not act within the
scope of application of the cited provisions. Therefore, it does not
have to be decided whether a circumvention of the law that is unlawful
under US law automatically means that the collection of evidence also
does not meet minimum standards for the rule of law. This at least
appears doubtful.

With regard to the collection of evidence in the unknown EU
member state, the constitutional complaint argues that the surveillance
did not occur in pursuit of a legitimate aim and that this automatically
leads to a violation of the principle of proportionality, which forms
part of the minimum standards for the rule of law. Because this state
did not conduct their own analysis of the data, so the complainant
argues, it merely provided ‘support’ to the USA and thus did not pursue
any aims of its own. Further, the passing on of this data to the USA
occurred in violation of the existing law on mutual assistance, as the
exchange of data between the USA and EU member states by way of mutual
legal assistance is limited to the passing on of data for the purposes
of prosecution and prevention of criminal offences, in accordance with
the ‘Agreement between the United States of America and the European
Union on the protection of personal information relating to the
prevention, investigation, detection, and prosecution of criminal
offences’ (Official Journal EU no. L 336 of 10 December 2016, p. 3 ff.).
Here, the complainant contends that there is no indication that the USA
used or intended to use the collected ANOM-data for this purpose.
Because of the geo-fence that had been put in place, data which
concerned the USA was not even transmitted.

This argument is also not coherent. The constitutional complaint
already fails to sufficiently substantiate that criminal prosecution of
individuals affected by the surveillance in the USA was not possible
under US law because of a lack of a link to the USA. First and foremost,
however, the constitutional complaint does not address the question of
whether the data collected which concerned the USA was relevant in
proceedings in the USA against individuals who were involved in the
development and marketing of ANOM-data in the belief that it was a
functioning encrypted messaging service. The complainant himself shows
that such criminal proceedings did take place in the USA. Consequently,
it does not have to be decided here whether the violation of the rules
of mutual legal assistance between the USA and the unknown EU member
state assumed by the constitutional complaint – which appears doubtful –
would have been capable of indicating a failure to meet the minimum
standards for the rule of law.

Insofar as the complainant claims that, due to the lack of
information available about the surveillance of ANOM-devices, he did not
have sufficient opportunity to influence the course and outcome of the
proceedings, his argument also does not establish a prohibition against
the use of evidence for constitutional reasons. In fact, the complainant
did have sufficient opportunity to influence the course and outcome of
the criminal proceedings against him because he was able, at any given
time, to make a statement regarding the communications surveillance
affecting him and, in particular, to call into question the authenticity
of those communications - which, tellingly, he explicitly does not. But
even if one were to take the view of the complainant that a lack of
information about the proceedings for collecting evidence abroad could
lead to a prohibition against the use of evidence for constitutional
reasons, there was no such lack of information in the present case. As
shown above, even based on the claims of the complainant, there is only
a lack of information regarding the identity of the EU member state
which hosted the iBot server as well as the orders of the courts in that
state on which the retention and transmission of the data to the USA was
based. However, this only concerns the question whether the retention
and transmission of data was permissible in accordance with the national
law of that state. According to the standards laid out above, this is
not relevant for the question whether a prohibition against the use of
evidence exists in Germany.

The analysis of the Federal Court of Justice that the violation
it found with regard to Art. 31 of the Directive regarding the European
Investigation Order in criminal matters did not lead to a prohibition
against the use of evidence under the so-called ‘weighing of interests’
in the present case is unobjectionable under constitutional law. It is
particularly relevant in this regard that in accordance with the
analysis of the ordinary law by the Federal Court of Justice, which is
unobjectionable under constitutional law, the requirements for
telecommunications surveillance in Germany under § 100a of the Code of
Criminal Procedure were clearly fulfilled. Especially in light of this,
the violation of § 91g(6) of the International Mutual Assistance Act
(Gesetz über die internationale Rechtshilfe in Strafsachen -
IRG) alleged in the constitutional complaint – which the Chamber merely
presumes – does not lead to a prohibition against the use of evidence
for constitutional reasons. That administrative bodies or courts could
have intentionally disregarded their legal obligations in this context
is not apparent on the basis of the claims made in the constitutional
complaint.

b) A violation of the right to one’s lawful judge, which the
complainant also continues to allege, is not possible on the basis of
the claims made by the complainant. Based on those claims, the Federal
Court of Justice did not act in a manifestly untenable manner in regard
to its duty of referral to the CJEU.

aa) Where a German court fails to comply with its duty to refer a
matter to the CJEU for a preliminary ruling, the right to one’s lawful
judge guaranteed to the person seeking legal protection in the initial
proceedings may be violated (cf. BVerfGE 73, 339 <366 et seq.>;
126, 286 <315>; 135, 155 <231 para. 177>). Yet the Federal
Constitutional Court only intervenes in relation to interpreting and
applying provisions governing the allocation of jurisdiction between
courts if the relevant provisions are interpreted and applied in a
manner that no longer appears comprehensible and is therefore considered
manifestly untenable when critically appraising the Basic Law’s central
notions (cf. BVerfGE 29, 198 <207>; 82, 159 <194>; 126, 286
<315>; 135, 155 <231 para. 179>).

The duty of referral under Art. 267(3) TFEU is applied in a
manifestly untenable manner where – in the view of the relevant court –
an issue of European law is decisive in proceedings before a court
against whose decisions no judicial remedy is available, yet the court
does not even consider a referral despite having doubts how to correctly
resolve the decisive issue and thereby develops European Union law on
its own authority (fundamental disregard of the duty of referral;
cf. BVerfGE 82, 159 <195>; 126, 286 <316>; 128, 157
<187>; 129, 78 <106>; 135, 155 <232 para. 181>; 147,
364 <380 para. 41>).

This also applies if case-law of the CJEU exists, but it appears
possible that the existing case-law does not exhaustively address the
decisive issue, or that further development of the jurisprudence by the
CJEU is more than just a remote possibility (incomplete case-law). In
such cases, Art. 101(1) second sentence of the Basic Law is violated
where the court against whose decisions no judicial remedy is available
untenably exceeds the margin of assessment necessarily afforded to it
(cf. BVerfGE 82, 159 <195 f.>; 126, 286 <317>; 128, 157
<188>; 129, 78 <106 f.>; 135, 155 <232 f. para. 183>).
This applies, in any case, if the ordinary courts arbitrarily assume the
existence of an acte clair or an acte éclairé. In this
respect, it is incumbent upon the court to sufficiently research the
relevant substantive European Union law. Where case-law of the CJEU is
applicable to the case at hand, it must be analysed and reflected in the
decision rendered by the court (cf. BVerfGE 82, 159 <196>; 128,
157 <189>; 135, 155 <233 para. 184>). On this basis, when
applying and interpreting the relevant substantive European Union law
(cf. BVerfGE 135, 155 <233 para. 184>), the ordinary court must
reach the reasonable conclusion that the applicable legal standards are
either clear from the outset (acte clair) or clarified beyond
reasonable doubts in the case-law of the CJEU (acte éclairé;
cf. BVerfGE 129, 78 <107>; 135, 155 <233 para. 184>). In the
case of incomplete case-law, Art. 267(3) TFEU is applied in an untenable
manner, particularly when the ordinary court concludes that the legal
situation is clear from the outset or clarified beyond reasonable doubts
without providing objective reasons supporting its conclusion
(cf. BVerfGE 82, 159 <196>; 135, 155 <233 para. 185>; 147,
364 <381 f. para. 43>).

bb) In accordance with these standards, the Federal Court of Justice
was not required to refer to the CJEU the question of a possible
prohibition against the use of evidence (under European Union law) in
case of a violation of the duty to provide information under Art. 31 of
the Directive regarding the European Investigation Order in criminal
matters or a violation of Art. 14(7) of the Directive regarding the
European Investigation Order in criminal matters.

With regard to prohibitions to use evidence in case of a violation of
the duty to provide information under Art. 31 of the Directive regarding
the European Investigation Order in criminal matters, the CJEU has
already expressed its point of view in its decision of 30 April 2024
(cf. CJEU, M.N. <EncroChat>, 30.04.2024, C-670/22, EU:C:2023:817,
paras. 107 ff., 124 f.). The Federal Court of Justice expressly based
its decision, which is challenged here, on this case-law of the CJEU
(cf. Federal Court of Justice, Judgment of 9 January 2025 - 1 StR 54/24
-, juris, para. 23). The constitutional complaint shows no deviation
from the case-law of the CJEU on a decisive issue and nothing more than
a remote possibility that the CJEU will further develop its
jurisprudence. In any case, the constitutional complaint does not
indicate that the Federal Court of Justice has exceeded its margin of
assessment.

A violation of the duty of referral to the CJEU with regard to a
possible prohibition against the use of evidence under European Union
law due to a violation of Art. 14(7) second sentence of the Directive
regarding the European Investigation Order in criminal matters is
already precluded by the fact that the Federal Court of Justice
considered this question not to be decisive in the present case. Even if
the Federal Court of Justice had applied this provision, according to
its findings, no violation would have been found. This is because in the
present case, the Federal Court of Justice affirmed that the proceedings
had been fair overall and that the rights of the defence had been
sufficiently safeguarded.

Finally, the Chamber points out that, independent of the
complainant’s submissions in the present case, it does not currently
have any information about the collecting of ANOM-data that could
provide indications for the assumption that the data obtained is in
principle inadmissible as evidence under constitutional law.

In accordance with § 93d(1) third sentence of the Federal
Constitutional Court Act, no further reasons are given.

As the constitutional complaint is not admitted for decision, the
application for a preliminary injunction becomes moot (§ 40(3) of the
Rules of Procedure of the Federal Constitutional Court).

This decision cannot be appealed.

Langenfeld

Fetzer

Offenloch

--- SUMMARY ---

The use of information gathered through the surveillance of ANOM communications, obtained by way of mutual legal assistance from the USA, as evidence in criminal proceedings in Germany does not raise constitutional concerns.

I. The applicant was convicted by a Regional Court and received a prison sentence. The factual findings supporting the conviction were almost entirely on the basis of evidence from text messages from encrypted ANOM communications that could be attributed to the applicant. The Federal Court of Justice rejected the applicant’s subsequent appeal on points of law as unfounded to the extent that it challenged the use of ANOM data as evidence.

With his constitutional complaint, the applicant asserts, inter alia, that the challenged decisions violate his right to a fair trial in accordance with the rule of law. He contends that the Regional Court and the Federal Court of Justice did not sufficiently consider whether the procedures to obtain the ANOM data, which occurred outside of Germany, complied with constitutional principles that grant a minimum level of fundamental rights protection and are indispensable under Article 79.3 in conjunction with Article 20.3 of the Basic Law. In addition, the applicant argued that the lack of transparency regarding the remote monitoring of devices with ANOM communications capability meant that he was unable to raise any objections to the data collection.

II. 
The constitutional complaint is inadmissible. The applicant has not coherently demonstrated the possibility of a violation of a fundamental right or a right equivalent to a fundamental right.

To the extent that the applicant contends that the ordinary courts did not sufficiently consider whether the procedures to obtain ANOM data complied with constitutional principles and provided a minimum level of fundamental rights protection because ‘nearly nothing is known’ regarding the means by which the data was obtained, this is contradicted by the complaint’s own statement of the facts. According to this, it is known that at the direction of the FBI, the software on ANOM devices was equipped with a ‘backdoor’ function that ensured that every message sent via the messenger service was sent to a so-called iBot server without the user’s knowledge, in the form of an encrypted copy that could be uniquely matched to the respective device. These messages would then first be decrypted by the FBI in a temporary storage on that server, then re-encrypted into that storage and finally forwarded to the transfer server with a few days’ delay. It is also known that a still unknown EU Member State made the iBot server available for the storage of messages on the basis of a bilateral mutual legal assistance treaty with the USA and transmitted the messages to the USA without decrypting and evaluating them itself. According to the findings of the ordinary courts, this process was conducted by the third country on the basis of court orders obtained in accordance with its procedural law.

The only information lacking is the identity of the state which hosted the server and the specific circumstances and content of the court orders. However, it is not clear why this information is relevant to the present case. While a review of these orders may have provided information as to whether the retention and transmission of the data to the USA complied with the law of the unknown EU Member State, that information is ultimately not relevant. If there are no indications that the foreign state to which the request for legal assistance was made violated the principles of the rule of law and the protection of human rights, it must be assumed that no such violation occurred.

The additional assertion of the applicant that the procedures to obtain evidence both in the USA and the unknown EU Member State did not meet the minimum requirements of the rule of law and also lacked the indispensable standards of fundamental rights protection is not supported by his factual submissions.

Among other things, the constitutional complaint seeks to substantiate a violation of the minimum requirements of the rule of law with the argument that conducting the surveillance of ANOM devices in a state other than the USA could only have been done to evade the limitations imposed by the Fourth Amendment of the US Constitution (protection of privacy) and by the Wiretap Act, which the applicant contends would have applied if communications infrastructure in the USA had been used. This argument is not sound. If the provisions of US law cited by the applicant only apply – as the applicant claims – in case of access to communications infrastructure in the USA, then a case of telecommunications surveillance that took place outside of this infrastructure must be outside their scope of application from the outset.

With regard to the obtaining of evidence in the unknown EU Member State, the constitutional complaint alleges that the surveillance did not serve a legitimate purpose and therefore violated the principles of proportionality which belong to the minimum standards of the rule of law. This argument is also unsound. The constitutional complaint does not substantiate that a criminal prosecution of persons affected by the surveillance in the USA according to US law would not have been possible due to the lack of connection to the USA. Above all, the constitutional complaint does not address the question of whether the data obtained was relevant for the USA in regard to criminal proceedings brought against individuals in the USA who participated in the development and marketing of ANOM in the belief that it was a functional encrypted messenger service.

In addition, to the extent that the applicant contends that he did not have sufficient opportunity to influence the course and result of the criminal proceedings against him because of insufficient transparency regarding the surveillance of ANOM devices, these claims do not result in a constitutionally mandated prohibition against the use of such evidence.

Independent of the applicant’s submissions, the Chamber does not currently have any information about the collecting of ANOM data that could provide indications for the assumption that the data obtained is in principle inadmissible as evidence under constitutional law.
